retrieved_docs.title: Substernal Thyroidectomy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Full history taking and clinical examination should be conducted in patients with suspicion of intrathoracic goiter, followed by thyroid function and antibody tests. Surgical planning before substernal thyroidectomy should include fiberoptic nasendoscopy to assess the airway and vocal cord status. [7] Although ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology is the most useful clinical tool to evaluate thyroid nodules in cervical goiters, this does not apply to extracervical goiters because of its difficulty in conductance and low risk of occult thyroid cancer. [4] Proper surgical management requires a multiplanar CT to plan the most suitable surgical technique. CT scanning, or MRI scanning rarely, helps determine the goiter anatomy and relationship to the adjacent structures, which are the trachea, esophagus, aortic arch, and great vessels. It also helps recognize possible tracheal obstruction and pathological lymph nodes. Finally, it is the work of a multidisciplinary team, including an endocrinologist, anesthetist, oncologist, head and neck, and thoracic surgeons, to take part in a discussion of possible oncological cases. [4]

Differentiate between open and minimally invasive thyroidectomy techniques to select the best approach for each patient. Coordinate with different care team members, creating a comprehensive preoperative and postoperative multidisciplinary care plan for patients undergoing thyroidectomy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Indications -- Type of Thyroid Resection
retrieved_docs.content: The specific circumstances dictate the decision to perform a total or partial thyroidectomy. Generally, diagnostic procedures (like repeat atypia of uncertain significance or Bethesda 4 lesions) and low-risk tumors (such as small papillary thyroid cancers without high-risk features) can be effectively treated with a thyroid lobectomy. More aggressive tumors, bilateral disease, hyperthyroidism, or goiter may require a total thyroidectomy. Based on the initial thyroid lobectomy specimen analysis, patients who undergo a thyroid lobectomy might need a completion thyroidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroidectomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Primary thyroid malignancy: Papillary thyroid cancers larger than 1 cm or with high-risk features should undergo surgery. Follicular thyroid cancers should undergo surgery. However, the diagnosis of follicular carcinoma can often only be made after a thyroid lobectomy. Differentiating between a benign follicular adenoma and a follicular thyroid carcinoma requires pathologic evidence of capsular or vascular invasion, which is only possible on a thyroid lobectomy specimen. All patients with undifferentiated tumors, oncocytic, medullary, and anaplastic thyroid cancers, require a total thyroidectomy at the minimum. More extensive resection and lymph node dissections may be indicated in certain situations. Hyperthyroidism: Medically uncontrolled hyperthyroidism due to autoimmune thyroiditis, toxic multinodular goiter, or toxic adenomas can be corrected with total thyroidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Parathyroid Minimally Invasive Surgery(Archived) -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Frozen section analysis is commonly used to confirm that the specimen removed is, in fact, parathyroid tissue. While a frozen section of a single gland cannot delineate an adenoma from multi-gland hyperplasia, the sensitivity of distinguishing parathyroid from the non-parathyroid tissue is greater than 99%.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Thyroid Gland -- Tissue Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Tissue preparation for immunohistochemistry requires surgically resecting the thyroid lesions. The preparation of thyroid samples includes fixation using 10% zinc formalin, after which the tissue requires embedding in paraffin. The embedding protocols vary according to the substrate, whether the protocol uses a chromogenic or fluorescent substrate. Paraffin embedding provides an option for the long-term preservation of tissues. The tissues must not be left to fix for more than 24 hours because over-fixation could mask the antigen. If necessary, tissues can get transferred to alcohol following fixation before the embedding process. Contrarily, the tissues can be frozen instead of fixing. Snap freezing is usually done to detect post-translational modifications such as phosphorylation, nitration, etc. Tissue freezing can be done by immersing tissues in liquid nitrogen or isopentane or by snap freezing in dry ice. Frozen tissue sections require alcohol fixing. This process avoids the need to retrieve epitopes that get masked by the formaldehyde/ formalin fixing method.

retrieved_docs.content: The term استئصال الدرقية is the Arabic name for thyroidectomy, a surgical procedure that involves removing all or part of the thyroid gland; it may be performed as a partial or a complete thyroidectomy depending on the underlying thyroid condition being treated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.content: may be identified in relation to the thyroid gland, reflecting pituitary‑thyroid axis cross‑talk. Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

Parathyroid exploration is captured by parathyroidectomy procedures, which may be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. Collectively, these diverse surgical techniques illustrate how the umbrella term “exploration procedure” integrates multiple organ‑specific operations, each with its own methodological variations and optional adjunctive steps, to facilitate diagnostic assessment, tumor removal, ductal clearance, or decompression in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (thyroid disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). The broader procedure category thyroidectomy; partial or complete serves as a parent concept for both استئصال الدرقية (total thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). Partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with دراق (the thyroid gland) itself, reflecting the close anatomical and therapeutic relationship between the gland and its surgical removal. This network of relationships underscores that thyroid cancer is viewed within the spectrum of thyroid disorders and that its management commonly involves either total or partial thyroidectomy, each linked to the underlying thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

This integrated view supports comprehensive patient care by connecting diagnostic, therapeutic, and hormonal aspects of endocrine and related gynecologic conditions.
